questions.question: Explain the role of an operating system as a resource manager. How does it manage competing requests from multiple processes?
keyPoints: OS allocates and deallocates CPU time among processes
Memory management tracks which portions are in use
I/O device scheduling and allocation
Prevents deadlock and starvation
Ensures fair and efficient resource sharing

questions.question: Differentiate between a system call and a system program with appropriate examples.
keyPoints: System call is a programming interface to kernel services
System programs are applications bundled with OS
Examples of system calls: open(), read(), fork()
Examples of system programs: file managers, text editors
System calls operate in kernel mode, system programs in user mode

questions.question: Describe the bootstrap process in an operating system. What is the role of the bootstrap program?
keyPoints: Bootstrap is the initial startup process
Stored in ROM/EEPROM
Performs POST (Power-On Self-Test)
Locates and loads OS kernel into memory
Transfers control to kernel, initializes system components

questions.question: What are the main advantages of a layered operating system structure? Explain briefly.
keyPoints: Modularity allows independent development of layers
Simplifies debugging as each layer can be tested separately
Changes in one layer do not affect other layers
Provides clear abstraction and separation of concerns
Easier maintenance and modification of system

questions.question: Explain the difference between user mode and kernel mode in operating systems. Why is this distinction necessary?
keyPoints: Kernel mode has full hardware access and privileged instructions
User mode has restricted access for security and stability
Prevents user programs from directly accessing hardware
System calls provide controlled transition between modes
Protects system from malicious or erroneous user programs

questions.question: List and briefly explain five essential services provided by an operating system to user programs.
keyPoints: Program Execution: Loading and running programs
I/O Operations: Managing input/output devices
File System Manipulation: Creating, reading, writing files
Communication: Data exchange between processes
Error Detection: Identifying and reporting errors
Resource Allocation: Managing CPU, memory, and devices

questions.question: What is multiprogramming? How does it improve CPU utilization in a computer system?
keyPoints: Multiprogramming keeps multiple programs in memory
When one program waits for I/O, CPU executes another
Increases CPU utilization by reducing idle time
Creates illusion of parallel execution
Maximizes throughput of the system

questions.question: Explain the different types of operating systems: Batch, Time-sharing, Real-time, and Distributed. Give appropriate use cases for each type.
keyPoints: Batch OS: Groups similar jobs, processes without user interaction (payroll processing)
Time-sharing OS: Multiple users share CPU, rapid switching (mainframes, terminals)
Real-time OS: Guaranteed response time for critical tasks (medical devices, industrial control)
Distributed OS: Multiple computers work together as single system (cluster computing)
Comparison of characteristics: response time, throughput, complexity

questions.question: Describe the structure of an operating system. Explain the main components and their functions in detail.
keyPoints: Process Management: Creating, scheduling, terminating processes
Memory Management: Allocating and deallocating memory space
File Management: Organizing and controlling access to files
Device Management: Controlling all I/O devices through drivers
Protection and Security: Ensuring authorized access to resources
System Call Interface: Gateway between user programs and kernel
Command Interpreter: Accepting and executing user commands

questions.question: Explain the concept of system calls in operating systems. Discuss the types of system calls and provide examples of each type.
keyPoints: System calls provide interface between user programs and OS kernel
Process Control: fork(), exec(), wait(), exit()
File Management: open(), read(), write(), close()
Device Management: readdev(), writedev(), ioctl()
Information Maintenance: getpid(), alarm(), sleep()
Communication: pipe(), socket(), msgget()
Protection: chmod(), umask(), chown()

questions.question: Discuss the monolithic and microkernel operating system structures. Compare their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Monolithic: All OS services in kernel, single large program
Advantages: Fast execution, direct communication between components
Disadvantages: Complex, difficult to maintain, any bug can crash system
Microkernel: Minimal kernel with services in user space
Advantages: Modular, reliable, easy to extend, better fault isolation
Disadvantages: Performance overhead due to message passing
Examples: Linux (monolithic), MINIX (microkernel)
